segments.s018-g89: جهاندار is a vocative/nominal address meaning 'sovereign' (here addressing the king). سی سال is a duration phrase 'thirty years'. از این بیشتر is a comparative/time phrase literally 'more than this', so together سی سال از این بیشتر means 'more than thirty years' (duration). Word order places the addressee before the temporal phrase for emphasis.
segments.s018-g90: چه گونه is the interrogative adverb 'how' ('in what manner'). پدید آوریدی is a compound past-tense verb (پدید + آوردن) meaning 'you brought forth / created / displayed' (2nd person singular past); پدید here is part of the compound rather than a standalone intransitive. هنر is the direct object 'art/skill' placed at the end in typical SOV order.
segments.s018-g91: برفت is a past-tense verb meaning 'he went away / departed' (the form with the prefix ب- is a poetic/classical past). و is the conjunction 'and'. سر آمد is a verbal phrase 'came/arrived' or 'came to an end'; بر او is the prepositional phrase 'upon him', and روزگار ('time' / 'fate') is the thing that 'came upon him' — word order is inverted for poetic emphasis.
segments.s018-g92: همه is a universal determiner 'all' modifying رنج (here 'suffering, hardship'). او is the possessive pronoun 'his' following the noun (typical Persian order: noun + pronoun). ماند is the past tense 'remained/was left'. از او یادگار literally 'a memorial from him' functions as a genitive phrase 'a memorial of him' (i.e. what remains to remember him by).
segments.s018-g65: A noun phrase listing the agents. همه (all/every) precedes the phrase as a quantifier; نره qualifies دیوان as 'male/virile' (an epithet attached to the noun), and افسونگران is joined by و (and). In classical/poetic order adjectives or epithets may follow or precede the head; here نره+دیوْن denotes 'virile/male demons.'
segments.s018-g66: برفتند is the past tense, 3rd-person plural verb 'they departed/went away.' The following nouns (جادو سپاهی گران) are a verbless nominal phrase specifying what accompanied/characterised them: جادو 'magic' and سپاهی گران 'a heavy/weighty host of soldiers' (سپاهی = soldiers/warriors, گران = heavy/formidable). Poetic word order places the verb before this descriptive nominal cluster.
segments.s018-g67: A compact descriptive noun phrase in apposition: دمنده (the blower) is the head, modified by سیه (black). دیوشان is دیو + the enclitic شان marking 'their' (here used to mean 'the demons' collectively) and پیش‌رو functions as a postpositive epithet 'foremost/forerunner' or 'vanguard.' The whole string names 'the black blower, the foremost of the demons.'
segments.s018-g68: همی is a poetic/adverbial particle meaning 'continually/kept on' and precedes the verb. بآسمان is a prepositional phrase 'to (the) sky,' برکشیدند is the past 3rd-person plural 'they raised up,' and غو is the object 'uproar/tumult.' Word order (adverb + PP + verb + object) reflects poetic inversion: 'they kept raising an uproar to the sky.'
segments.s018-g69: A nominal title + name with a postpositive epithet: جهاندار is a title 'sovereign/ruler' placed before the king's name طهمورث (Tahmūras), and بافرین ('with praise' = 'praiseworthy, glorious') is an epithet applied to him. Classical Persian often places such epithets after the name.
segments.s018-g70: بیامد is the past tense 'he came.' کمربسته is a participial adjective 'girt/girded' (lit. having girded his loins) meaning 'armed/ready.' جنگ و کین is a coordinated noun phrase 'war and vengeance' stating his purpose or state. The sequence gives 'he came, girded for war and vengeance.'
segments.s018-g71: یکایک is an adverb 'one by one' placed before the verb. بیاراست here is a perfective verb 'he/she/it arrayed/armed (himself),' with the agent understood from context. با marks the adversary ('with') and combines with دیو 'demon' and جنگ 'battle' to mean 'to do battle with the demon' or 'for battle against the demon.'
segments.s018-g72: نبد is an archaic negative/existential verb meaning 'there was not' or 'did not occur.' It negates the following noun phrase جنگشان را ('their battle' with the object/topic marker را). فراوان modifies درنگ and here means 'much' or 'abundant,' so the collocation implies 'their fighting had not much pause' — i.e. there was hardly any delay in their battle. The particle را is the definite/object marker attached to جنگشان.
segments.s018-g81: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. آزاد گشتند is a past-tense, plural verbal phrase ('they became free'), where گشتند is the perfective/change-of-state verb 'became' and آزاد is the predicate adjective. از بند او is a prepositional phrase 'from his bond/fetter' (از + noun + enclitic pronoun او).
segments.s018-g82: بجستند is a past plural transitive verb 'they sought'. ناچار functions adverbially here ('necessarily/inevitably') modifying the verb. پیوند او is the object 'his bond/union/alliance' with the pronoun او following the noun; the object marker را is omitted (a common poetic omission).
segments.s018-g83: نبشتن is the infinitival noun 'writing / to write' used as the thing taught. به خسرو is the dative phrase 'to Khosrow' (به marking the indirect object). بیاموختند is a past plural transitive verb; here it means 'they taught' (not 'they learned'). Word order (object + indirect object + verb) is typical in classical Persian.
segments.s018-g84: دلش را is the direct object 'his heart' formed by the noun دل + possessive enclitic ش and the definite object marker را. به دانش is an oblique/benefactive phrase 'with / to knowledge'. برافروختند is a past plural causative/inchoative verb 'they kindled' used metaphorically to mean 'they aroused/instilled' (knowledge kindled the heart).
segments.s018-g85: نبشتن is again the noun-infinitive 'writing/script'. یکی نه is an emphatic negation 'not one' (literally 'one — no'), and the particle که introduces the corrective/explanatory clause 'but/that' which here leads into the quantity statement. نزدیک سی means 'about/approximately thirty' (نزدیک used as 'nearly').
segments.s018-g86: The repeated چه ... چه ... is a correlative/enumerative formula meaning 'whether/be it ... or ...' used to list alternatives or examples. رومی, تازی, پارسی name the languages/orthographies (Rûm/Roman, Arab, Persian). و is the coordinating 'and' linking the list.
segments.s018-g87: Again, the repeated چه introduces a list of languages/types ('whether Sogdian, Chinese, or Pahlavi'). Each language name is a noun labeling a script or tongue; the construction emphasizes variety rather than asking a question.
segments.s018-g88: ز is the poetic/prepositional 'from/of'. هر گونه‌ای is the indefinite expression 'every kind / whatsoever sort'. کان is an archaic/classical particle introducing a relative clause ('that/which'), here serving to link to the verb; its specific sense is not listed in the provided sense choices. همی is the classical continuous/modal particle used with present forms, and بشنوی is the second-person singular present/subjunctive 'you hear' (i.e. 'that you may hear' / 'that you hear').
segments.s018-g33: بفرمودشان is a past tense verb phrase (ب + فرمود + شان) meaning 'he commanded them.' تا introduces purpose ('so that'/'in order that') and is followed by the present-subjunctive نوازند, here functioning as a jussive/purpose clause 'so that they play.' گرم is an adverbial adjective modifying نوازند, meaning 'fervently/with warmth.' Word order places the verb-form at the end of the clause as is normal in Persian.
segments.s018-g34: نخوانندشان is the negative present-subjunctive form (ن + خوانند) with the enclitic object -شان 'them' and here means 'they should not sing/recite/call them.' جز به introduces an exception 'except with,' where به here works like 'with/by' and آواز نرم is a prepositional phrase 'a gentle/soft voice.' Note the verb carries the enclitic object directly (نخوانند-شان).
segments.s018-g35: چنین گفت is 'he said thus' (چنین = 'thus'), introducing reported speech. The ک (که) here links the quote to the reporting verb and what follows; کاین را ستایش کنید is an admonition in the present-subjunctive/imperative addressed to plural 'you' (ستایش کنید = 'praise [it]'). را marks the definite direct object کاین ('this/that').
segments.s018-g36: جهان‌آفرین را نیایش کنید is a parallel imperative clause: جهان‌آفرین ('the world-creator/Creator') is the object marked by را, and نیایش کنید is the verbal phrase 'perform prayer/offer worship' (نیایش as noun + کردن in conjugated form used as 'pray'). The object-before-verb order is normal in Persian.
segments.s018-g37: که introduces an explanatory clause 'for/that.' او دادمان is a past-tense verb with the enclitic مان 'to us' meaning 'he gave us.' بر is the preposition 'on/upon/over' and governs the following noun phrase; دستگاه here is likely 'dastgāh' (a musical mode/system) and functions as the thing given. Word order places the giver and the indirect object in front of the preposition phrase.
segments.s018-g38: ستایش مر او را is an inverted, slightly archaic ordering meaning roughly 'praise to him from me' (ستایش = 'praise'; مر = 'me'/'by me'; او را marks the person receiving praise). The following که بنمود راه is a relative clause 'who showed the way' (بنمود = past 'showed', راه = 'way'), modifying او. Together this yields 'praise to him from me who showed the way.'
segments.s018-g39: مر او را places the speaker's perspective first ('to me' / 'for me') with او را marking the person. یکی پاک دستور بود is a nominal predicate: یکی (a certain / one) modifies پاک (pure) which modifies دستور (instruction/guide/order); بود is the past copula 'was.' The whole phrase says 'he was, for me, a single/pure guide.'
segments.s018-g40: که introduces a relative clause referring back to the previous noun (his guide). رایش is 'his counsel/judgement' and ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; کردار بد = 'bad/evil conduct'; دور بود is 'was far/removed.' Together: 'whose counsel was kept far from evil deeds.' Word order places the possessed noun (رایش) before the complement introduced by ز.
segments.s018-g9: جهان is the direct object 'the world/universe'. از بدی‌ها is an ablative phrase 'from evils' (با/از + plural noun). بشویم is a first‑person volitional/optative verbal form used poetically as 'I will cause to be washed/cleanse' (ب‑ + شو(دن) used here with an active/transitive sense); به رای is an instrumental/agentive phrase 'by/with counsel' modifying the action.
segments.s018-g10: پس آنگه are adverbial particles ('then' / emphatic then). کنم is first‑person singular present/future 'I will do / I will make' used as the main verb 'I will set up'. درگهی is the object 'a royal court' (note the archaic -ی nominal form). گرد پای is a locative phrase 'around/at (my) feet' (گرد = 'about/around' + پای = 'foot/feet'), placed after the object for a poetic word order.
segments.s018-g11: ز هر جای is a prepositional phrase 'from every place' (ز used poetically like از). کوته کنم is a causative verbal phrase where کوته (short) is used as an object complement 'I will shorten / cut short' and کنم supplies the action 'I will make/cause'. دست دیو is the direct object 'the demon's hand' (دست + noun modifier دیو).
segments.s018-g12: که introduces a result/purpose clause 'so that / that'. من is the subject 'I'. بود + خواهم here form a periphrastic/archaic expression: بود functions as the verbal base 'be' and خواهم gives volition/future, combining to mean roughly 'that I shall be / may become'. جهان را is the definite object with را marking the realm, and خدیو (khedive) names the office — overall: 'that I shall be khedive of the world.'
segments.s018-g13: هر آن چیز is a stock phrase 'every that thing / everything'. کاندر is an archaic fused relative 'که + در' meaning 'which is in / that is within', so کاندر جهان سودمند = 'which in the world is useful'. سودمند is a qualifying adjective 'useful/profitable' modifying چیز.
segments.s018-g14: کنم is the speaker's verb 'I will do / I will cause' used with emphasis. آشکارا is an adverb 'openly' modifying the following verb. گشایم (from گشاد/گشا) is first‑person subjunctive/volitional 'I will open/loosen' (here 'free, set free'), and ز بند is an ablative phrase 'from bondage/fetter' indicating what it is freed from. Together: 'I will openly loosen / free (them) from bonds.'
segments.s018-g15: پس 'then' is a temporal adverb. از پشت is the prepositional phrase 'from the back(s)'. میش و بره are coordinated nouns 'ewe and lamb' (the sources), and پشم و موی are the paired goods 'wool and hair' taken from them; coordination with و is standard.
segments.s018-g16: برید is a past action verb here 'they cut / they took (sheared)'. و 'and' links clauses. به رشتن نهادند is a causative/periphrastic past: نهادند (they placed/put) with the verbal noun/infinitive رشتن 'to spin' = 'they set (it) to spinning'. روی at the end functions adverbially 'upon / on' (i.e., upon that / to be spun). The order places the past verb بریـد before the coordinated periphrasis for a compact poetic sequence.
segments.s018-g49: همه راه is an adverbial phrase 'in every way / on every road'; نیکی is the direct object 'goodness'; نمودی is the simple past (2nd singular) of نمودن (literary form of 'to show' / 'to do'), so the clause reads literally 'you showed goodness to the king'; به شاه is the indirect object ('to the king'). Word order is adverbial/object/verb/indirect-object, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s018-g50: همه modifies راستی and here means 'entire/complete' (not 'everyone'); خواستی is the past tense (2nd sg) of خواستن 'you desired/wanted'; پایگاه is the noun object 'base/foundation' (here 'support' or 'grounding' for the uprightness). The clause therefore means 'you desired complete uprightness as a foundation.'
segments.s018-g51: چنان = 'thus/so'; شاه is the subject; پالوده گشت is a compound predication: پالوده (past-participle/adjectival 'purified') + گشت (past of گشتن) = 'became purified'. از بدی uses از to mark separation/cause: 'purified from evil' / 'rid of evil.' This is a literary past passive-like construction ('he became purified').
segments.s018-g52: که introduces a result/explanatory clause ('so that' / 'such that'); تابید is past (3rd sg) of تابیدن here meaning 'shone'; ازو = 'from him' (arch. enclitic form of از او); فره is the subject 'farr / divine glory' and ایزدی is its adjective 'divine.' Word order places the verb before its noun phrase for a poetic effect: 'that (so) shone from him the divine farr.'
segments.s018-g53: This clause contains two past actions tied together: the first verb (برفت) is a literary past-form meaning roughly 'was driven off / took off / departed' (subject is implied from prior context), followed by the direct object اهرمن را (Ahriman, with را marking it definite). به افسون is an instrument phrase 'by/with enchantment'; ببست is past 3rd-singular 'he bound / tied'. The sequence gives '(he) drove off Ahriman and bound him with enchantment.' Note: برفت here is a poetic/literary verb form; the subject is omitted and recovered from context.
segments.s018-g54: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'; the phrase بر تیز رو بارگی is a poetic compound: تیز رو ('swift-faced' / 'swift-running') modifies بارگی (here 'the beast/pack/thing that bears a load' — i.e. a steed or mount), and the preposition بر before نشست ('sat') marks location/direction. نشست is the past (3rd sg) 'he sat/settled.' So: 'when (he) sat upon the swift-running mount.' The order is preposition + adjectival phrase + noun + preposition + verb, typical of older verse.
segments.s018-g55: زمان تا زمان is an idiomatic repetition meaning 'from time to time / era to era'; زینش is زین + ـش 'his/its saddle' (possessive enclitic); بر ساختی is the verb ساختی (past 2nd sg 'you made') with بر marking the directional/causative relation ('you made on/for it'). So the clause reads 'from time to time you fashioned its saddle.'
segments.s018-g56: همی is an adverb 'still/always/continually' placed before the verb for emphasis; گرد here means 'around/about'; گیتی‑ش is گیتی (the world) + ـش (a bound pronoun referring back to the subject) so 'around the world' (or 'around his world' in poetic reference); بر تاختی is تاختی (past 2nd sg 'you rode/charged') with preposition بر used before the verb. The whole means 'and continually you rushed/rode around the world.'
segments.s018-g17: به کوشش is an adverbial prepositional phrase meaning “with effort” (به + noun), placed before the verb to give manner. از او marks the source/material ("from it/him"). کرد پوشش is the past-tense verbal construction (کرد + noun) meaning “made a covering” (کرد here = made/caused). به رای, placed after the verb phrase, means “according to plan/with counsel” and modifies the manner or motive of making the covering.
segments.s018-g18: به گستردنی uses به + verbal-noun (the -نی nominalization) to mean “for/to the spreading” (purpose/instance). بد modifies رهنمای and means “bad/poor” here. هم is the adverb “also/too,” emphasizing addition. او رهنمای is a verbless nominal clause (typical in Persian poetry) meaning “he was the guide” (the copula 'بود' is omitted); together: “as for spreading it, he too was a poor guide.”
segments.s018-g19: ز پویندگان is an ablative phrase “from/of the poyandegān” (poyandegān = those who run/search — trackers/hunters). هر چه functions together as “whatever/whichever,” introducing an indefinite relative (هر = any/whichever, چه = whatever). بد تیز رو is a compound adjective: تیز‌رو means “fast-moving/quick,” and بد here intensifies or characterizes it as ‘badly/very’ (an archaic/poetic usage); overall: “whatever swift-running (ones) of the trackers.” Word order (modifier after noun) is typical of Persian poetic compression.
segments.s018-g20: خورش here means ‘food/fodder’ (a noun). کردشان is a contracted verbal phrase: کرد (made/did) + ـشان (their/for them) giving “made for them” or “gave them.” The list that follows (سبزه و کاه و جو) enumerates the fodder items: grass/green growth, straw, and barley — the direct objects of خورش/کردشان. The verb phrase is past tense, and the order (object then verb+clitic) reflects poetic inversion.
segments.s018-g21: رمنده is an adjective meaning “driven away/fleeing” modifying ددان (here: wild beasts/game). را is the definite-object marker marking ددان as the direct object. همه follows to mean “all” (they looked over all of the driven-off beasts). بنگرید is an imperative or past/subjunctive-looking poetic form of نگر (to look/inspect) used here as the main verbal action (“they inspected/looked at”).
segments.s018-g22: سیه گوش is a compound noun (سیه = black/dark, گوش = eared/ear) naming one type of animal; و joins it with یوز (the cheetah). از میان is a prepositional phrase “from among,” and برگزید is the past-tense verb “chose/selected.” Word order is straightforward: objects named, source phrase, then verb.
segments.s018-g23: به چاره is a purpose phrase: به + noun چاره “for a remedy/for a solution/for taming/training” (here: bringing into training). بیاوردش is the past tense verb بیاورد (bring) plus the object clitic -ش (him/it/them) meaning “brought him/it/them.” از دشت و کوه specifies origin “from the plain and the mountain.” The verb is past tense and the object clitic attaches directly to the verb, a common classical/Persian construction.
segments.s018-g24: به بند آمدند is a passive/resultative expression meaning “they came into bondage/they were fettered” (بند = bond/fetter, آمدند = came/were brought). آن‌که is a relative pronoun “those who / the ones that.” بد zān گروه qualifies which ones: بد = “bad/ill” and زان (ز + آن) = “from that/of that,” so آن‌که بد زان گروه = “those of that group that were bad.” The clause therefore says the bad ones of that group were brought into bonds.
segments.s018-g25: ز مرغان is a prepositional/partitive phrase meaning “of the birds / among the birds.” آن را که introduces a relative clause “that which / who,” and بد نیک are contrasting adjectives (“bad, good”) placed before تاز, a poetic verbal root meaning “rush/swoop” (here describing manner). The short word مر in this line is archaic/uncertain in function and does not map neatly to the modern senses given.
segments.s018-g26: چو … چو is a pair of similes: چو باز و چو شاهین = “like the hawk and like the falcon.” گردن‌فراز is a compound adjective placed after the simile, literally “neck‑raising,” i.e. “proud‑necked / holding the head high.” The coordinator و simply links the two similes.
segments.s018-g27: بیاورد is 3rd singular past “he brought/fetched.” و is the coordinator “and.” آموختن‌شان is the infinitive “to teach them” (آموختن) plus the object enclitic -شان “them.” گرفت is past “took/ seized” used idiomatically with an infinitive to mean “took to (doing)” → “began to.”
segments.s018-g28: جهانی here means “the whole/world at large” (subject), بدو is the dative pronoun “to him/at him,” مانده is the past participle/adjective “remained,” and اندر شگفت is a prepositional phrase “in wonder/astonishment.” Word order places the result (“remained in wonder”) after the dative.
segments.s018-g29: چو این کرده شد is a temporal subordinate clause: چو = “when,” این کرده = “this having been done,” شد is the auxiliary/past marker making the clause “when this had been done.” ماکیان و خروس are the nouns “poultry (domestic fowl) and the cock” (subjects of the following rhetorical question).
segments.s018-g30: کجا opens a rhetorical question “where / how (could…)?” بر + خروشد: بر is a prepositional/coverb particle used with the verb خروشد (to make a loud outcry) — together meaning “raise an outcry (against/at).” گه is an adverb “occasionally / sometimes,” and زخم کوس is a genitive phrase “the drum’s blow/strike” (زخم used metaphorically for a striking blow of the war‑drum, کوس).
segments.s018-g31: بیاورد is again 3rd singular past “he brought.” و = “and.” یک‌سر is an adverb here meaning “all at once / wholly.” به مردم is a prepositional phrase “before/to the people.” کشید is the past of کشیدن “to draw/present” — here idiomatically “displayed / set forth” (the exact supplied sense list did not include this verbal sense).
segments.s018-g32: نهفته (hidden) qualifies what follows; همه = “all.” سودمندش is “its useful/profitable (ones),” with the enclitic -ش referring back to the things/birds shown; گزید is the past “he chose/selected.” Word order places the adjectives before the verb for emphasis: “he selected all that was hidden and useful.”
segments.s018-g1: پسر بد is a noun + adjective ('son' + 'bad/evil') in the normal noun‑then‑adjective order. مر is the old/dialectal dative/benefactive pronoun 'to/for me' placed before the object pronoun او را ('him' + definite object marker ra). یکی هوشمند is a small nominal phrase 'a certain/one intelligent (man)', where یکی ('a certain') modifies the adjective هوشمند, and this phrase functions as a predicate or appositive identifying the person just mentioned.
segments.s018-g2: گرانمایه (an epithet) precedes the proper name طهمورث and together form 'the esteemed Tahmuras.' دیو بند is a compound epithet: دیو 'demon' + بند (here used nominally as 'fetter/binder') = 'binder of demons' or 'demon‑fetter.' Word order is epithet → name → further epithet.
segments.s018-g3: بیامد is a simple past/past narrative 'he came.' The prepositional phrase به تخت پدر ('to/onto his father's throne') appears before the verb نشست ('sat'), and بر here gives the sense 'upon/on' (بر + نشست = 'sat upon'). The verb نشست is the past tense 'he sat/settled.'
segments.s018-g4: به شاهی is a prepositional/goal phrase 'to/for kingship' (indicating purpose or office). کمر بر میان‌بر ببست is an idiomatic sequence meaning 'he girded his belt (for action/for kingship)': کمر here = 'girdle/belt', بر = 'on/upon', میان‌بر is used as the item/position (here 'mid‑belt / expedient' in older usage) and ببست is the past 'he bound/tied' (from بستن). Together they express the action of girding oneself to take up rule.
segments.s018-g5: همه موبدان را is the definite object 'all the mobeds' marked by را. ز لشکر is a prepositional phrase 'from the army/host' (ز = 'from' in poetic usage). بخواند is a past narrative/performative verb here meaning 'he summoned/called' (classic Persian بخواند = 'called/summoned' in this narrative register).
segments.s018-g6: به خوبی is an adverbial phrase 'well/with care.' چه مایه is an interrogative/indefinite phrase meaning 'what sort/what means' (مایه = means/resource). سخن‌ها is the plural 'words/speeches' and براند (from راندن) here functions as the verb governing that phrase; in context it means roughly 'to set forth/advise/decide upon' (the lexical list gives 'drive/drive away', but here it is used in the sense of carrying out or proposing counsel). The whole clause gives the purpose/content of the summons.
segments.s018-g7: چنین گفت is a fixed reporting formula 'thus said' (introducing direct speech). کامروز is an adverb 'today/this very day' that immediately follows the reporting phrase. تخت و کلاه are the two nouns 'throne and hat/crown' that begin the reported predication. The phrase order is the standard narrative introduction to direct speech.
segments.s018-g8: مرا زیبد is a short predication 'befits me / suits me' where مرا is the object pronoun 'me' (+ را split in the provided word list) and زیبد (from زیبیدن) is a classical present/past‑stative meaning 'to become/ to befit.' این تاج و گنج و سپاه is the enumerated list 'this crown and treasure and army' that the speaker says befit him; the list is the logical subject/topic of زیبیدن in reported speech.
segments.s018-g57: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the clause. The verb بدیدند (past, 3pl) follows the subject دیوان (here 'demons'); کردار او is the object phrase 'his deed(s)/conduct' with the possessive pronoun او following the noun as in standard Persian word order.
segments.s018-g58: کشیدند (past, 3pl) + گردن is an idiomatic sequence meaning 'they drew/bent their necks' (i.e. bowed/low­ered their heads). The preposition ز ('because of, from' in poetry) introduces the cause گفتار او 'his speech/words'. Word order: verb + object (گردن) then prepositional phrase (ز گفتار او).
segments.s018-g59: شدند (past, 3pl) here introduces an intransitive event 'there arose/assembled'. انجمن دیو بسیار is the noun phrase 'a great assembly of demons' (انجمن = assembly, دیو = demon, بسیار = many). مر is an archaic/dialectal dative/benefactive 'for me' (it marks that the assembly came 'for/around me').
segments.s018-g60: که introduces a relative/clausal modifier describing the assembly or those demons. پردخته مانند is an adjectival phrase 'appearing/formed as if well‑wrought' (پردخته = well‑made, مانند = like/as if). از او marks source 'from him' and is followed by the nouns تاج و فر 'crown and royal splendour' (از + noun indicates origin or taken-from).
segments.s018-g61: چو 'when' introduces the temporal clause. طهمورث is the subject name; آگه شد is the compound predicate 'became aware' (آگه = aware, شد = became). از کارشان is a prepositional phrase 'of/their deeds' with از marking the object of awareness and شان the third‑person plural possessive.
segments.s018-g62: بر آشفت is a classical/periphrastic expression meaning 'he rose up (against them)' — بر here gives the sense 'against/upon' and آشفت is the past participial/stative root 'became agitated/rose'. The coordinated clause و بشکست بازارشان uses بشکست (past, 3sg) 'he broke/destroyed' with بازارشان 'their market/bazaar' as object.
segments.s018-g63: به فر جهاندار is a prepositional phrase 'to/onto the sovereign's glory' (به = to/onto, فر = royal glory, جهاندار = sovereign). بستش is the past transitive 'he bound/fastened it' where the enclitic -ش refers back to the item being bound; میان ('middle') indicates the location ('in the middle' / 'at the centre'). Note classical ordering: prepositional phrase + verb + enclitic object + locative.
segments.s018-g64: به گردن بر آورد is a regular Persian construction where بر آورد (past, 3sg) 'he brought/put on' takes به گردن 'onto the neck' as its locative/complement. گرز گران is the object noun phrase 'a heavy mace' with the adjective گران following the noun (typical Persian noun‑adjective order).
segments.s018-g41: خنیده is a verbal adjective/past participle meaning 'sung' or 'chanted' and here functions as the main predicative action. به هر جای is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'in/at every place' placed before the name it modifies. شهرسپ نام places the proper name (شهرسپ) with نام ('name' or 'fame') — classical Persian often puts the name next to نام, so this phrase means 'the name Shahrasp' (or his fame).
segments.s018-g42: نزد here carries the sense 'in the eyes/presence of' (i.e., 'among/with people'). جز به نیکی is the idiom 'except for goodness' (جز = except; به = 'for'/'by'); the following به هر جای گام means 'at every step / wherever he went' with گام 'step' giving the sense 'wherever he set foot'. The clause therefore says that, among people, there was nothing except good repute wherever he went.
segments.s018-g43: همه روز is an adverbial phrase 'all day'. بسته here is an adjectival/predicative form 'closed/sealed' and combines with ز + infinitive (ز خوردن) to mean 'kept closed from eating' — a poetic way to say he fasted or kept his mouth shut. دو لب (two lips) is the affected body-part phrase; word order is inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s018-g44: به پیش جهاندار = 'before/in the presence of the sovereign' (به پیش = before, جهاندار = sovereign). بر پای شب is a compact classical phrase meaning 'throughout the night at (his) feet' or 'standing at the feet during the night' (بر پای = at the foot/of the feet; شب gives the time). Together the segment describes night vigil before the king.
segments.s018-g45: چنان is an adverb 'so/thus'. بر دل هر کسی literally 'on the heart of every person' = 'in everyone's heart'. بود is the past copula 'was', and دوست is used predicatively here meaning 'beloved' or 'a friend' — together: 'So was he beloved in every heart.' Note the classical word order (adverbial + locative + copula + predicate).
segments.s018-g46: نماز شب و روزه is a coordinated noun phrase 'night prayer and fasting.' آیین اوست is the copular clause 'are his custom/practice' (آیین = custom/practice; اوست = 'it is his' / 'he is'). The whole says these devotions formed his regular practice.
segments.s018-g47: سر مایه is a compound meaning 'chief source' or 'root/cause' (سر = 'head/chief', مایه = 'cause/resource'). بد اختر is an attributive phrase 'ill‑star' (astrological misfortune) modifying the source. شاه را uses the classical object/definite marker را after 'king' to indicate that the source belongs to or concerns the king ('the king's ...').
segments.s018-g48: در بسته is a locative/predicative phrase 'enclosed/hidden in' (در = in; بسته = closed/sealed), used to say where that source was. بد جان is an adjectival compound 'ill‑souled / evil‑hearted' modifying بدخواه ('ill‑wisher'); را again marks that ill‑wisher as the definite/person at issue. The clause thus says the cause lay hidden in the sealed, evil‑souled enemy.
segments.s018-g73: This clause literally reads 'of them two shares by/enchant­ment he bound.' از ایشان ('of/from them') identifies the group; دو بهره is a quantified phrase 'two portions/two of them'; به افسون is an instrumental phrase 'by/with a spell' (به used as 'by'); ببست is a past tense transitive verb 'he bound/tied' with the object previously marked by از ایشان and دو بهره.
segments.s018-g74: دگرشان ('the others', with شان = clitic ‘them’) is the object; the prepositional phrase به گرز گران ('with a heavy mace') gives the instrument (به here = 'by/with'); کرد is the past-tense verb 'he made/did' and پست is the resultative adjective 'low/abased' — literally 'he made the others low by a heavy mace.'
segments.s018-g75: کشیدندشان is a past plural verb 'they dragged/pulled them' with the clitic شان attached to the verb as object. The following adjectives خسته و بسته خوار form a series modifying that object: 'weary and bound, humiliated.' Word order places the verb+object before the adjectival descriptive phrase (typical in narrative style).
segments.s018-g76: به جان خواستند is an idiom: به جان + خواستند = 'they begged/implored for their lives' (به = 'for/to', جان = 'life', خواستند = 'they asked/wanted'). آن زمان 'at that time' gives the temporal setting. زینهار is an interjection/exclamation here (a cry of alarm or plea).
segments.s018-g77: This is a plea in the imperative/negative: ما را مکش is 'do not kill us' (ما را = 'us' with object marker را; مکش is the negative imperative form). The conjunction تا introduces purpose/result: تا یکی نو هنر = 'so that (you may learn) one new art.' Word order puts the negative imperative before the purpose clause.
segments.s018-g78: بیاموزی is a present-subjunctive/optative form 'may you learn' (used after تا for the purpose). از ما 'from us' marks the source. که‌ت is a contracted relative + enclitic (که + ـت) = 'which/that to you'; آید is a present or subjunctive 'comes' (i.e. 'that will come'); به بر is a short prepositional phrase meaning 'to/for (your) benefit' — together: 'that you may learn from us one new art which will come to you.'
segments.s018-g79: کی نامور here functions as a proper name/title (Kay Namvar, a royal name in the Shahnameh) and stands as the subject. دادشان is 'gave them' (داد + شان, the clitic marking the indirect object), and زینهار is a noun 'warning/admonition' — 'Kay Namvar gave them the warning.'
segments.s018-g80: بدان here is the compound form (ب- prefix + دان) functioning like 'concerning that' or 'to that end'; تا introduces a purpose clause 'so that.' نهانی is adverbial/adj. 'secret/secretly' and کنند is the present-subjunctive/optative 3rd-person plural 'they may do/make' (used after تا for purpose). آشکار stands as the complement 'manifest/openly' — the clause expresses the intended result/purpose (keep it secret and then bring it to light).

sentences.s018-s45: O sovereign, how did you bring forth (or display) skill for more than thirty years?
sentences.s018-s46: He departed, and his time (fate) came upon him; all his suffering remained as a memorial of him.
sentences.s018-s33: All the virile demons and enchanters withdrew — a mighty magical host.
sentences.s018-s34: The black blower — the demons' vanguard — continually raised an uproar toward the sky.
sentences.s018-s35: The sovereign Tahmūrath, famed, came girded for war and vengeance.
sentences.s018-s36: One by one he arrayed himself to fight the demon; their fighting had scarcely any pause.
sentences.s018-s41: When they were freed from his fetter, they inevitably sought his alliance.
sentences.s018-s42: They taught Khosrow to write, and they kindled his heart with knowledge.
sentences.s018-s43: Not a single script, but nearly thirty — Roman, Arab, and Persian.
sentences.s018-s44: Whether Sogdian, Chinese, or Pahlavi — of every kind that you might hear.
sentences.s018-s17: He commanded them to play with fervor, and not to sing except with a gentle voice.
sentences.s018-s18: He said thus: 'Praise this, and pray to the Creator.'
sentences.s018-s19: For he granted us a dastgāh (a musical mode/system), — praise (be) to him from me who showed the way.
sentences.s018-s20: To me he was a single, pure guide — his counsel was far from evil conduct.
sentences.s018-s5: I will cleanse the world of evils by my counsel; then I will set up a court at my feet.
sentences.s018-s6: From every place I will cut short the demon's hand, so that I may become the khedive (ruler) of the world.
sentences.s018-s7: Everything in the world that is useful, I will openly free from bondage.
sentences.s018-s8: Then from the backs of ewe and lamb they took wool and hair, and they set it to spinning.
sentences.s018-s25: In every way you showed goodness to the king; you sought complete uprightness as your base.
sentences.s018-s26: Thus the king was purified of evil, so that the divine glory shone from him.
sentences.s018-s27: He drove off Ahriman and bound him with enchantment; when he mounted the swift steed he sat down.
sentences.s018-s28: From time to time you made its saddle, and continually you rode around the world.
sentences.s018-s9: With effort they made from it a covering; as for spreading it, he too was a poor guide.
sentences.s018-s10: Of the trackers, whatever swift-footed (animals) they had, they made their food of grass, straw and barley.
sentences.s018-s11: They inspected all the driven-off beasts and chose, from among them, the black‑eared one and the cheetah.
sentences.s018-s12: He brought them for taming from the plain and the mountain; those of the group that were bad were put in bonds.
sentences.s018-s13: Among the birds was that one which, whether bad or good, swooped like a hawk and like a falcon, proud-necked.
sentences.s018-s14: He brought them and began to teach them; the whole world was left amazed at him.
sentences.s018-s15: When this was done, how could the domestic fowl and the cock raise a clamor when at times the drum’s blow resounded?
sentences.s018-s16: He brought them and displayed them at once to the people; he selected all their hidden useful ones.
sentences.s018-s1: He had an ill (or wicked) son; to me (he was) a certain wise one — the esteemed Tahmuras, binder of demons.
sentences.s018-s2: He came and sat upon his father's throne; he girded his loins for kingship.
sentences.s018-s3: He summoned all the mobeds from the host, to consider well what counsels (words) to pursue.
sentences.s018-s4: Thus he said: 'Today a throne and crown befit me — this crown, treasure, and army.'
sentences.s018-s29: When the demons saw his deeds, they bent their necks because of his words.
sentences.s018-s30: A vast assembly of demons gathered for me, bearing crowns and royal splendour as if fashioned from him.
sentences.s018-s31: When Tahmuras became aware of their deeds, he rose up against them and smashed their market.
sentences.s018-s32: He fastened it to the sovereign's glory at the middle, and hung a heavy mace about his neck.
sentences.s018-s21: In every quarter they chanted the name 'Shahrasp'; among people there was nothing but goodness wherever he set foot.
sentences.s018-s22: All day his two lips were closed from eating; by night he stood at the sovereign's feet.
sentences.s018-s23: Thus he was beloved in every heart; night prayer and fasting were his practice.
sentences.s018-s24: The root cause of the king's ill‑star lay hidden in the sealed, ill‑souled ill‑wisher.
sentences.s018-s37: He bound two of them with a spell; he made the others low with a heavy mace.
sentences.s018-s38: They dragged them, exhausted and bound, despised; at that time they begged for their lives, crying 'Zinehār!'
sentences.s018-s39: Do not kill us, so that you may learn from us one new art that will come to you.
sentences.s018-s40: Kay Namvar gave them that warning so that they would keep it hidden and then (later) make it manifest.
